California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
Natasha Merkuloff
city Adninistrator
city of calistoga
1232 washington street
calistoga, cA 94515
April 17, L99t
Re: Your Request for Informal Advice
Our File No. I-9 0-6 7 7
Dear Ms. Merkuloff:
You have requested advice on behalf of calistoga city Mayor,
Jin Hughes, concerning his duties under the confl ict-of-interest
provisions'of the Political Reform Act (r'the Act").
-specifically, you have asked whether Mayor Hughes rnay participate
in decisions regardinq the Resource Management Systen ordinance,
or portions theieof . 2 -
OUESTION
The Resource Management System (nRMSx) is designed to address
the problens associated with linited water resources and limited
serei cap.city in the city of Calistoga. A proposed revised
ordinance is now before the city council.
Do the conf I ict-of-interest 
provisions of the Act prohibit
Mayor Hughes from making, participating in rnaking, or in. any $/ay
atlenpting to use his official 
position to influence decisions
concerning the RMS ordinance, or portions thereof?
1 Gor".r,."nt code Sections 81ooo-91015. A11 statutory references
are to the covernment code unless otherr.rise indicated. 
Commissi-on
regulations apPear at 2 California code of Regulations. section
18oOO, et seq. A11 references to regulations are to Title 2,
oivision 6 of the california code of Regulations.
2 8"".,r=" you have not asked for advice about a specific pendj-ng
decision, wt are treating your request as one for informal
assistance. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor
with the inmunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 cal. code of Regs. Section
18329 (c) (3) , coPY enclosed. )
Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 95804-0807 . (916\322-5660
428 J


Fi■e No. I-90-677
Page 2
CONCLUS ION
Whether Mayor Hughes has a conflict of interest under the Act
which requires his disqual ification wil-1 depend on the particular
city counciL decision and the decision's effect upon his interests
in real property and upon the sources of income to him.
FACTS
Mayor Hughes ovrns a real property residence located at 1004
cedar Street, Calistoga, and a real property rental of $1,000 or
more Located at 1oo4A cedar street, cal,istoga. The tenant of
1oo4A Cedar Street, Polly Thacher, is a source of rental income to
uayor Hughes .
calistoga Rea1ty company al-so is a source of income to Mayor
Hughes by virtue of his wife's employrnent with that company.
Other sources of incorae to Mayor Hughes are lenders. These are
Napa Val1ey Bank and Surety Savings.
In 1989, the Calistoga city council directed the Sewer and
i,later Advisory Comrnittee (swAC) to prepare an update to the
Resource Management System ordinance. on JuIy 18, 1990, the
proposed reviied ordinance was reviewed by the Calistoga Planning
commission. It is now before the Calistoga Ci'ty council. staff
is recommending that the city councit approve a negative
declaration and adopt the RMS ordinance with proposed amendments
to the appeal section.
one of the issues of the RMS is the allocation of water for
different uses, residential, commercial, and industrial. 
Another
issue of the RMS is that it gives preferences in terrns of vrater
and sewer a1]ocations to low to noderate housing projects.
ANALYSTS
The Act prohibits any public officiat 
from making,
participating in rnaking, or otherwise using his or I'er official
position to inftuence a governmental decision in which the
officiat 
has a financial interest. 
(Sections 8710o and 87103.)
The Act provides a five-part test to determine whether a
public official 
has a conflict of interest in a governnental
decision. First, is the off icj,al, considering rnaking, participat-
ing in rnaki.ng, or using hj,s or her official position to j'nfluence
a governrnentaf decision? (Section 87100.) second, does the
ofricialicial h".re any economj-c interests in the governmental decision?
(Section 871-03.) Third, is it foreseeabl'e that the decision wilf
affect the official's 
economic interests? (Id.) 
Fourth, is the
effect of the decision on the official's 
economi.c interests
material? (rd.) 
Fifth, is the effect of the decision on the
officiaf's 
economic interests distinquishable fron its effect on
the public qenerallv? (Id.) 
Each of these elernents must be met
before there i.s a conflict of interest.


File No. I-90-677
Page 3
1. Makino or Participatinq In A Governmental Decision
An official 
nakes a governmental, decision when the official
votes, commits hj.s or her agency to a course of action, enters
into a contract, or appoints someone. (Regulation 187oo(b) r coPY
enclosed.) Participating in the naking of a governmental decision
inctudes, among other things, advisinq or makinq recommendations
to the decision maker. (Regulation 18700(c).) An official
attempts to use his or her official position to influence a
decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the
official 
contacts or appears before, or otherwise attempts to
influence, any nenber, officer, enployee or consultant of the
agency. (Regnllation 18700.1(a).) If uayor Eughes has a conflict
oi interest, he is prohibited from atl these actions.
2. Econonic Interests
The second issue is whether the official 
has any econornic
interests in the governmental decision. Section 87103 provides
that an official 
has a financial interest in a decision if it is
reasonabJ,y foreseeable that the decisi.on will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable fron its effect on the pubJ-ic
generally, on the official 
or a member of his or her immediate
familyr or on:
(b) Any real property in which the public
official, has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand doLLars ($1,000) or more.
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a conmercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official, status' aggregating two hundred fifty
dolLars ($250) or rnore in value provided to,
received by or prornj.sed to the public official
within 12 months prior to the tine when the
decision is made.
Section 87103 (b) - (c) .
Mayor Hughes has several potentially disqualifying economic
interests: 
his real, property interests (Section 87103(b)) and
sources of income to hirn of S25o or more within the prior 12
nonths (Section 87103(c)). Income includes outstanding loans and
his comrnunity property share of his wife's incorne. (Section
82030(a).) Income does not include his salary from the County of
Solano. (Section 8203o (b) (2) . )
3    An officia■
′s tlimmediate family'l inc■udes his or her spouse
and dependent children.  (SectiOn 32029.)


FiIe No. a-9o-677
Page 4
3. Foreseeable Financial Effect on An Economic Interest
The third issue is the foreseeability that the decision will
affect Mayor Hughes' economic interests. 
The effect of a decision
is reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood
that it wiLl occur. To be reasonably foreseeable, the effect of a
decision must be more than a mere possibility, 
however certainty
is not required. 
(Downev cares v. Downev communitv DeveloDment
Com. (1987) 196 CaI.App.3d 983, 989-991; Witt v. Morrow (1977) 7o
cal.App.3d 817i In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198, copy
enclosEa. ) The Act seeks to prevent more than actual conflicts of
interest; it seeks to prevent even the appearance of a possible
conf l-ict of interest. 
(Witt v. Morrow, suDra at 823.)
when a matter cones before Mayor Hughes and he suspects he
rnay have a conflict of interest, he should exarnine the matter to
determine whether any decision by hirn would have a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on any of the economic interests
listed in Section 87103. In Mayor Hughes' case, several econonic
interests may be affected by the decisions concerning the RMS
ordinance.
i./e note that the city of calistoga is presently under a sewer
and water moratorium. The RMs ordinance will make available city
water for new or expanded residentiaL, conmercial and industrial
uses. This nay nean that the real- estate market in the city of
CaJ,istoga wil,I be affected. It is reasonably foreseeabLe,
therefoie, that a decision concerning the RMs ordinance rnay
increase or decrease the value of calistoga Realty Company, his
wife's employer.
Furthermore, we note that decisions concerning the aLlocation
of vrater and waste water resources may be applied in a way to
restrict growth and, as such, arguably could increase existing
residential property values. (weidnan Advice Letter, No. A-9o-
21lr copy enclosed. ) Thus I'tayor Hughes will need to apply the
following tests to each of his econornic interests-
4, Material Financial Effect
The Commission has adopted a series of regulations to
deterrnine whether a financial effect is rnaterial. In order to
apply the regutations in a particular case, it is necessary to
foot- at the economic interest affected and then examine both the
direct and i,ndirect effects of the decision on those interests.
ff the effect of the particular decision is material- under any of
the following criteria, 
the public official 
may have a conflict of
interest.
A. Material Financial Effect on Real Propertv
f-) Direct Effect on Interests in Real ProDertv.
The effect of a decision will be naterial if the decision
directly involves real- Property in which the official 
has an
"r".rship interest of 91,ooo or nore. GeneralIy a decision will


File No. A-9O-677
Page 5 
t
directly involve the property if the decision concerns whether the
public official's 
property will be incl,uded in a zoning or
redevelopment area, a license for the use of the official's
property, or the taxes on the official's 
property. 
(Regulation
ta'|02.1, copy enclosed.) Regnrl-ation 18702.1(a) (3) provides a more
cornprehensive list of when a public officiaL's real property is
directly involved in a governnental decision.
2l Indirect Effect on Interests in ReaI Propertv.
ff Mayor Hughes' property wil,1 not be directly affected by
the decision, he must detennine lrhether his property nay be
indirectly affected. Regulation L8'7o2.3, (copy enclosed) sets
forth the standards for deterrnining whether the indirect effect of
a decision will naterially affect real property. That reguLation
provides in part:
(a) The effect of a decision is material as
to real property in which an official 
has a direct,
indirect or beneficial ownership interest (not
including a leasehold interest), if any of the
following applies:
(1) The real property in which the
official 
has an interest, or any part of that
real property, is located within a 3o0 foot
radius of the boundarj,es (or the proposed
boundaries) of the property which is the
subject of the decision, unless the decision
will have no financial effect upon the
officiaL's real Property interest.
(2) The decision involves construction
of, or irnprovements to, streets, water, sevrer,
storm drainage or similar facilities, 
and the
real property in which the official 
has an
interest will receive ne!, or substantially
improved services.
(3) The real property in which the
officiat 
has an interest is located outside a
radius of 300 feet and any part of the real
property is located wj.thin a radius of 2,5oo
feet of the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the property which is the
subject of the decision and the decision will
have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect
of:
(A) Ten thousand dollars (S10,o00)
or more on the fair market value of the
real property in which the official 
has
an interest; or


Fil-e No. l-90-677
Page 6
(B) will affect the rental value of
the property by $1,000 or Dore per 12
month period.
If the effect of the RMs ordinance decision on Mayor Hughes'
real property fa1ls within either subparagraphs (1) , (2), or (3)
above, the effect is material .
B. Material Financial Effect on Sources of Incorne
His spouse's employer, calistoqa Realty company, his tenant,
PoIIy Thacher, and several lenders are sources of income to llayor
Hughes. He should rernember that if any source of income of 9250
or more in the 12 months preceding the decision is directly or
indirectly involved in the decision, he nay have a conf 1j.ct of
interest.
1) Direct Effect on Sources of Income
The effect of a decision wil.t be material if the decision
directlv involves sources of income to the officiaL of $25o or
more in the preceding 12 nonths. Generally a decision will
directly involve a source of incorne if the source of income
initiates the proceeding or is the subject of the proceeding.
(Regulation L8702.1(b) . )
)\ 
Tndirect Effect on Sources of Incone
If the source of j.ncone to the official 
is a business entity,
Mayor Hughes woul-d apply Regulation 18702.2 (copy enclosed) .to
delermine whether an indirect effect of a decision is materi.al.
For the purpose of this analysis we wi1l, assume that his wife's
emptoyer, calistoga Realty company, is not listed on any stock
exchange or securities 1ist, is not a Fortune 50o conpanyr and
does not have net tangible assets of at least $4,ooo,ooo.'l Under
these assumptions Regulation lA7O2.2 provides, in part, that the
effect of a decision on calistoga Real-ty company is rnaterial if:
(1) The decision will resul-t in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenue for a fiscal year
of S1o,000 or more; or
(2) The decision wiII result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional, expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or nore; or
4 Hor.r.r, this assumption probably is not correct for the
Ienders, Napa Valley Bank and surety Savings, which presunably
have net tangible assets of at least $4,000,000 or above. The
standards to be applied in order to determine \.rhether any given
decision will have a material- financial effect on these sources of
incorne are contained in other subdivisions ((a)-(f)) 
of Regulation
La7 02 .2 .


File No. I-90-677
Page 7
(3) The decision will result in an increase
decrease in the val-ue of assets or liabilities
S10,0o0 or more.
Regulation ].87 02 .2 (q) .
If the source of income to the official 
is an individual,
Ivlayor Hughes would apply Regulation 18702.6 (copy enclosed) to
deterrnine whether an indirect effect of a decision is naterial.
That regulation provides that the effect of the decision on an
individuat, such as Po1ly Thatcher, j,s naterial if:
(a) The decision will affect the individual's
incorne, investrnents, or other tangible or
intangible assets or liabilities 
(other than real
property) by $r,ooo or more; or
(b) The decision wi1] affect the individual's
real property interest in a manner that is
considered material under section 18702.3 or
Section La1 02.4.
Regulation la7 02 .6 .
Mayor Hughes should exarnine the Iikely financial effect of
any RMs ordinance decision on sources of income to hirn and
delerrnine whether the effect meets the tests listed above.
If any of Mayor Hughes' economic i.nterests $/ould be
materially affected by any decision related to the revised RMs
ordinance, he would be requj.red to disgualify himself frorn
participating in these decisions unless the ttpublic generallyrl
exception applies.
5. 
t'PubI ic GeneralIv" ExceDtion
Even if the decision before }{ayor Hughes meets all the above
elements, disqua 1i f ication j.s required only if the effect upon his
econornic interests is distinguishabLe from the effect upon the
pubtic generally. 
(Section 87103.) The publicrr is all- the
persons residing, owning property, or doing business in the
jurisdiction of the agency in question. (In re Leqan, (1985) 9
Feec ops. L, 15, copy enctosed.) rn the case of a city council,,
this wouLd be the entire city. 
If the decision does not affect
atl the members of the public in the same manner, disqualification
may be required unless the effect of the decision is the same as
the effect on a significant seguent of the public. 
(Regulation
18703, copy enclosed.) Therefore, for the "public generallyrl
exception to app1y, any decision would have to affect a
significant segment of the city of Calistoga in substantially the
sane manner as it would affect Mayor Hughes' economic interest.
(Dowd Advice Letter, No. A-88-214; Burnham Advice Letter, No. A-
86-210 , copies encl,osed. )
ｒ
ｆ
Ｏ
　
Ｏ


File No. I-90-677
Page 8
I"layor Hughes' has tr.ro real property interests, his personal
residence and a rental. 
Decisions concerning the allocatj-on of
i'/ater and waste water resources may be applied in a way to
restrict growth and, as such, arguably could increase property
values. The rule regarding public officials 
owning three or fet'rer
rental units is enunciated in In re Ferraro (]-978) 4 FPPC 62 (copy
enctosed) . Ferraro provides that an official 
who owns three or
fewer residential units may participate in a decision affecting
his or her economic interests because the property interests of
such an official 
wil-I not be affected by the decision in a manner
that is distinguishabLe from the effect upon a significant segment
of the public AeneralLy. Therefore, since Mayor Huqhes owns only
one rentaL unit, he would not be required to disqualify hinself
from participati,ng in the RMS ordinance decision on account of his
real property interests.
on the other hand, one of Mayor Hughes' economic interests is
a source of income to hirn, Calistoga ReaLty Company, which may be
financialty affected and in a rnaterial, manner by a decision
concerning the RMs ordinance. We do not have sufficient
inforrnation to reach a conclusion on this. 
However, assuming thi,s
to be true, it does not appear likely that the ttpublic generallyrl
exception wiLl- be applicable to Mayor Hughes because the financial
effect of RMS decisions on Calistoga Realty Company is
distinguishable from the effect on the public Aenerally or on a
significant segment of the public. 
Therefore, Mayor Hughes may be
required to disqualify hinself frorn participating in RMs ordinance
decisions on account of this economic interest.
I hope this letter provides you with the guidance you
requested. If you have any further questions regarding this
matter, pfease do not hesitate to contact me at (91-5) 322-5901.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General counsel

subject of the decision, unless the decision
will have no financial effect upon the
officiaL's real Property interest.
(2) The decision involves construction
of, or irnprovements to, streets, water, sevrer,
storm drainage or similar facilities, 
and the
real property in which the official 
has an
interest will receive ne!, or substantially
improved services.
(3) The real property in which the
officiat 
has an interest is located outside a
radius of 300 feet and any part of the real
property is located wj.thin a radius of 2,5oo
feet of the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the property which is the
subject of the decision and the decision will
have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect
of:
(A) Ten thousand dollars (S10,o00)
or more on the fair market value of the
real property in which the official 
has
an interest; or


Fil-e No. l-90-677
(B) will affect the rental value of
the property by $1,000 or Dore per 12
month period.
If the effect of the RMs ordinance decision on Mayor Hughes'
real property fa1ls within either subparagraphs (1) , (2), or (3)
above, the effect is material .
B. Material Financial Effect on Sources of Incorne
His spouse's employer, calistoqa Realty company, his tenant,
PoIIy Thacher, and several lenders are sources of income to llayor
Hughes. He should rernember that if any source of income of 9250
or more in the 12 months preceding the decision is directly or
indirectly involved in the decision, he nay have a conf 1j.ct of
interest.
1) Direct Effect on Sources of Income
The effect of a decision wil.t be material if the decision
directlv involves sources of income to the officiaL of $25o or
more in the preceding 12 nonths. Generally a decision will
directly involve a source of incorne if the source of income
initiates the proceeding or is the subject of the proceeding.
(Regulation L8702.1(b) . )
)\ 
Tndirect Effect on Sources of Incone
If the source of j.ncone to the official 
is a business entity,
Mayor Hughes woul-d apply Regulation 18702.2 (copy enclosed) .to
delermine whether an indirect effect of a decision is materi.al.
For the purpose of this analysis we wi1l, assume that his wife's
emptoyer, calistoga Realty company, is not listed on any stock
exchange or securities 1ist, is not a Fortune 50o conpanyr and
does not have net tangible assets of at least $4,ooo,ooo.'l Under
these assumptions Regulation lA7O2.2 provides, in part, that the
effect of a decision on calistoga Real-ty company is rnaterial if:
(1) The decision will resul-t in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenue for a fiscal year
of S1o,000 or more; or
(2) The decision wiII result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional, expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or nore; or
4 Hor.r.r, this assumption probably is not correct for the
Ienders, Napa Valley Bank and surety Savings, which presunably
have net tangible assets of at least $4,000,000 or above. The
standards to be applied in order to determine \.rhether any given
decision will have a material- financial effect on these sources of
incorne are contained in other subdivisions ((a)-(f)) 
of Regulation
La7 02 .2 .
(3) The decision will result in an increase
decrease in the val-ue of assets or liabilities
S10,0o0 or more.
Regulation ].87 02 .2 (q) .
If the source of income to the official 
is an individual,
Ivlayor Hughes would apply Regulation 18702.6 (copy enclosed) to
deterrnine whether an indirect effect of a decision is naterial.
That regulation provides that the effect of the decision on an
individuat, such as Po1ly Thatcher, j,s naterial if:
(a) The decision will affect the individual's
incorne, investrnents, or other tangible or
intangible assets or liabilities 
(other than real
property) by $r,ooo or more; or
(b) The decision wi1] affect the individual's
real property interest in a manner that is
considered material under section 18702.3 or
Section La1 02.4.
Regulation la7 02 .6 .
Mayor Hughes should exarnine the Iikely financial effect of
any RMs ordinance decision on sources of income to hirn and
delerrnine whether the effect meets the tests listed above.
If any of Mayor Hughes' economic i.nterests $/ould be
materially affected by any decision related to the revised RMs
ordinance, he would be requj.red to disgualify himself frorn
participating in these decisions unless the ttpublic generallyrl
exception applies.
5. 
t'PubI ic GeneralIv" ExceDtion
Even if the decision before }{ayor Hughes meets all the above
elements, disqua 1i f ication j.s required only if the effect upon his
econornic interests is distinguishabLe from the effect upon the
pubtic generally. 
(Section 87103.) The publicrr is all- the
persons residing, owning property, or doing business in the
jurisdiction of the agency in question. (In re Leqan, (1985) 9
Feec ops. L, 15, copy enctosed.) rn the case of a city council,,
this wouLd be the entire city. 
If the decision does not affect
atl the members of the public in the same manner, disqualification
may be required unless the effect of the decision is the same as
the effect on a significant seguent of the public. 
(Regulation
18703, copy enclosed.) Therefore, for the "public generallyrl
exception to app1y, any decision would have to affect a
significant segment of the city of Calistoga in substantially the
sane manner as it would affect Mayor Hughes' economic interest.
(Dowd Advice Letter, No. A-88-214; Burnham Advice Letter, No. A-
86-210 , copies encl,osed. )
ｒ
ｆ
Ｏ
　
Ｏ
I"layor Hughes' has tr.ro real property interests, his personal
residence and a rental. 
Decisions concerning the allocatj-on of
i'/ater and waste water resources may be applied in a way to
restrict growth and, as such, arguably could increase property
values. The rule regarding public officials 
owning three or fet'rer
rental units is enunciated in In re Ferraro (]-978) 4 FPPC 62 (copy
enctosed) . Ferraro provides that an official 
who owns three or
fewer residential units may participate in a decision affecting
his or her economic interests because the property interests of
such an official 
wil-I not be affected by the decision in a manner
that is distinguishabLe from the effect upon a significant segment
of the public AeneralLy. Therefore, since Mayor Huqhes owns only
one rentaL unit, he would not be required to disqualify hinself
from participati,ng in the RMS ordinance decision on account of his
real property interests.
on the other hand, one of Mayor Hughes' economic interests is
a source of income to hirn, Calistoga ReaLty Company, which may be
financialty affected and in a rnaterial, manner by a decision
concerning the RMs ordinance. We do not have sufficient
inforrnation to reach a conclusion on this. 
However, assuming thi,s
to be true, it does not appear likely that the ttpublic generallyrl
exception wiLl- be applicable to Mayor Hughes because the financial
effect of RMS decisions on Calistoga Realty Company is
distinguishable from the effect on the public Aenerally or on a
significant segment of the public. 
Therefore, Mayor Hughes may be
required to disqualify hinself frorn participating in RMs ordinance
decisions on account of this economic interest.
I hope this letter provides you with the guidance you
requested. If you have any further questions regarding this
matter, pfease do not hesitate to contact me at (91-5) 322-5901.

The tenant of 1oo4A Cedar Street, Polly Thacher, is a source of rental income to uayor Hughes . calistoga Rea1ty company al-so is a source of income to Mayor Hughes by virtue of his wife's employrnent with that company. In 1989, the Calistoga city council directed the Sewer and i,later Advisory Comrnittee (swAC) to prepare an update to the Resource Management System ordinance. on JuIy 18, 1990, the proposed reviied ordinance was reviewed by the Calistoga Planning commission.
